frameworks.name: temporal discounting
frameworks.one_line: modified discounting model incorporating individual-specific risk sensitivity

frameworks.name: computational psychiatry
frameworks.one_line: computational markers as clinically validated indicators of addiction outcomes

core_question: Can a risk-sensitivity-adjusted temporal discounting measure reliably signal clinically relevant outcomes in opioid addiction treatment?
key_assumptions: impulsivity in addiction can be captured by intertemporal choice models
individual differences in risk sensitivity bias standard discount-rate estimates
discount rates can change longitudinally with treatment status
manipulations: model specification: standard discounting vs risk-sensitivity-augmented discounting
longitudinal assessment across treatment course in opioid-addicted patients
key_findings: risk-sensitivity-augmented model yields more precise, unbiased, and reliable impulsivity estimates than the standard model
within-patient changes in discount rate track increases in drug use
interpretations: risk-adjusted discount rate is a candidate computational marker of vulnerability to relapse and treatment failure in opioid addiction
